summary: Bryan Cantrill and Steve Tuck pay tribute to Charles Beeler, a venture capitalist who passed away from cancer. Beeler was an early investor in both Joyent and Oxide Computer, distinguished by his hands-on approach during difficult times and his genuine enthusiasm for creators and innovators. The post highlights how Beeler persistently encouraged people to do their best work, including pushing Cantrill to deliver a candid keynote that became one of his most meaningful talks.
key_points: Charles Beeler was an unusual VC who served on the ACM Queue board, bringing a different perspective to practitioner content
Unlike investors who hide during hard times, Beeler actively worked to fix problems at Joyent, including leading the search for new executive leadership
Beeler's persistence led to Cantrill's 'Platform as Reflection of Values' talk, pushing him to speak candidly about the strained Joyent/node.js relationship
Beeler invested in Oxide despite it being a challenging fit for his fund, working to address objections and winning over his partnership
money_quotes: Charles is one of the good ones, you know.
When companies hit challenging times, some investors will hide—but Charles was the kind of investor to figure out how to fix what was broken.
If the Joyent relationship with node.js had become strained, so much more the reason to speak candidly about it!
He worked behind the scenes to encourage people to do their best work, always with an enthusiasm for the innovators and the creators.
You were the best of venture capital. Closer to the heart, you were a terrific friend to us both.
key_insight: The best investors distinguish themselves not by picking winners but by rolling up their sleeves during hard times and persistently encouraging founders to do ambitious, honest work.
